question.text: Scenario: The Kapoor family consists of Mr. Rajiv (45, software engineer, gross salary Rs. 15 lakh p.a.) and Mrs. Neha (42, freelance designer, annual income Rs. 6 lakh). Their monthly breakdown is: Gross salary (Rajiv) Rs. 1,25,000, Freelance income (Neha) Rs. 50,000, Employer PF contribution Rs. 1,800, Employee PF contribution Rs. 1,800. Mandatory expenses: Tax deductions Rs. 12,000, Loan EMIs Rs. 35,000. Essential expenses: Groceries Rs. 15,000, Children's education Rs. 20,000, Rent Rs. 25,000, Utilities Rs. 8,000, Transportation Rs. 10,000. Discretionary expenses: Entertainment Rs. 8,000, Lifestyle Rs. 12,000, Annual holiday fund Rs. 5,000. Their assets include: Equity MFs Rs. 22 lakh, FDs Rs. 10 lakh, PPF Rs. 8 lakh, PF balance Rs. 14 lakh, Savings bank Rs. 2 lakh, Gold jewellery Rs. 6 lakh, Car (3 years old) Rs. 5 lakh. Liabilities: Home loan Rs. 18 lakh, Car loan Rs. 2,50,000. Question 5 of 5: The Kapoor family's debt-to-income ratio is:

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Monthly debt servicing = Rs. 35,000 (total EMIs). Monthly income = Rs. 1,78,600. DTI = 35,000 / 1,78,600 = 19.6%. **The Trap:** 25% might use only Rajiv's salary in the denominator. Use total family income. 29.2% might include tax as debt servicing — taxes are mandatory expenses but not debt servicing. **Cross-Exam Context:** DTI ratio is tested in X-A (Ch 3 and Ch 4). Below 35-40% is considered healthy. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** 19.6% DTI is well within safe limits. The Kapoors have room to take additional debt if needed (e.g., for children's higher education) but should avoid unnecessary borrowing.
